content:
# Exodus Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43v–44r (PDF pp. 81–82); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 58–59 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_017_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_page_002.jpg`

---

### The People Demand Water — "Is YHWH Among Us or Not?" (vv. 1–7)

**1** The whole Israelite community set out from the wilderness of Sin, traveling in stages as YHWH directed, and they camped at Rephidim. But there was no water for the people to drink.

**2** So the people quarreled with Moses. "Give us water to drink!" they demanded. Moses said to them, "Why are you quarreling with me? Why are you putting YHWH to the test?"

**3** But the people were desperate with thirst, and they complained against Moses: "Why did you bring us up out of Egypt — to kill us and our children and our livestock with thirst?"

**4** Moses cried out to YHWH, "What am I supposed to do with these people? They are on the verge of stoning me!"

**5** YHWH said to Moses, "Walk out ahead of the people. Take some of the elders of Israel with you, and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile. Go. **6** I will be standing there before you on the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock, and water will come out of it for the people to drink." Moses did this in full view of the elders of Israel.

**7** He named the place *Massah* and *Meribah* — "Testing" and "Quarreling" — because of the Israelites' quarreling and because they tested YHWH by asking, "Is YHWH among us, or not?"

---

### Amalek Attacks — Joshua's First Battle (vv. 8–13)

**8** Then Amalek came and attacked Israel at Rephidim.

**9** Moses said to Joshua, "Choose men for us, and go out tomorrow to fight against Amalek. I will stand on the top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand."

**10** Joshua did as Moses told him and went out to fight Amalek, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill. **11** Whenever Moses raised his hands, Israel prevailed; and whenever he lowered his hands, Amalek prevailed. **12** But Moses' hands grew heavy. So they took a stone and put it under him, and he sat on it. Then Aaron and Hur held up his hands — one on each side — so that his hands remained steady until the sun went down.

**13** And Joshua overwhelmed Amalek and his forces with the sword.

---

### YHWH-Nissi — A Perpetual War (vv. 14–16)

**14** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Write this down as a memorial in a document and recite it aloud to Joshua: I will completely blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven."

**15** Moses built an altar and named it *YHWH-Nissi* — "YHWH Is My Banner."

**16** He said, "A hand is raised to the throne of YH! YHWH will wage war against Amalek from generation to generation."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — REPHIDIM AND THE ABSENCE OF WATER (Heb. *vayyis'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el mimidbar-Sin lemase'eihem al-pi YHWH vayyachanu biRfidim ve'ein mayim lishtot ha'am*, וַ֠יִּסְע֠וּ כׇּל־עֲדַ֨ת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֜ל מִמִּדְבַּר־סִ֗ין לְמַסְעֵיהֶם֙ עַל־פִּ֣י יְהוָ֔ה וַֽיַּחֲנ֖וּ בִּרְפִידִ֑ם וְאֵ֥ין מַ֖יִם לִשְׁתֹּ֥ת הָעָֽם):** The phrase *lemase'eihem* (למסעיהם, "according to their journeys/stages," from *masa'*, מסע, "a pulling up of stakes, a stage of journey") indicates that the movement from Sin to Rephidim was not a single march but a series of encampments. The critical qualifier is *al-pi YHWH* (על פי יהוה, literally "upon the mouth of YHWH" = "at YHWH's command/direction"). The people traveled where YHWH told them to go — and YHWH led them to a place with no water. This is theologically explosive: God himself directed them to the place of crisis. The test at Rephidim is not accidental; it is designed.

The name *Rfidim* (רפידם) is etymologically uncertain. Some connect it to the root *rafad* (רפד, "to spread out, to support, to make a bed"), which would make it "resting places" or "supports" — ironic, since the place provides no support at all. Others derive it from *rafah* (רפה, "to be slack, to let drop"), which would anticipate the motif of Moses' sagging hands in vv.11-12. The location is generally placed in the southwestern Sinai Peninsula, near Wadi Feiran, close to Jebel Musa (the traditional Mount Sinai).

The sentence *ve'ein mayim lishtot ha'am* (ואין מים לשתת העם, "and there was no water for the people to drink") is a devastatingly simple statement. After the hunger crisis of chapter 16, which God resolved with manna, now comes the more existential crisis: thirst. A person can survive weeks without food but only days without water. The stakes are higher than chapter 16.

**v.2 — *RIV* AND *NASSAH* — QUARRELING AND TESTING (Heb. *vayyarev ha'am im-Mosheh vayyomru tenu-lanu mayim venishteh vayyomer lahem Mosheh mah-terivun immadi mah-tenassun et-YHWH*, וַיָּ֤רֶב הָעָם֙ עִם־מֹשֶׁ֔ה וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ תְּנוּ־לָ֥נוּ מַ֖יִם וְנִשְׁתֶּ֑ה וַיֹּ֤אמֶר לָהֶם֙ מֹשֶׁ֔ה מַה־תְּרִיבוּן֙ עִמָּדִ֔י מַה־תְּנַסּ֖וּן אֶת־יְהוָֽה):** Two key verbs define this verse and the entire episode. The first is *vayyarev* (וירב, from *riv*, ריב, "to quarrel, to contend, to bring a legal dispute"). The word *riv* is not merely an argument; it is a technical legal term — a formal accusation, a lawsuit. The people are not just complaining; they are bringing charges against Moses (and, through him, against God). This legal sense gives birth to the place-name *Meribah* (מריבה, "Quarreling/Contention") in v.7.

The second verb is *tenassun* (תנסון, from *nasah*, נסה, "to test, to put to the proof"). Moses' counter-question — *mah-tenassun et-YHWH* (מה תנסון את יהוה, "why are you testing YHWH?") — reframes the people's demand as a theological act. To demand that God prove himself by providing water is to *test* God — to make divine provision the condition of faith rather than the fruit of it. This gives birth to the name *Massah* (מסה, "Testing") in v.7. The verb *nasah* appeared in 15:25 and 16:4, where God tests Israel; now the roles are reversed — Israel presumes to test God.

The imperative *tenu-lanu mayim* (תנו לנו מים, "give us water!") uses the plural *tenu* (תנו, "give!"), addressed to both Moses and Aaron (or Moses collectively as leadership). The demand is blunt, almost violent — no politeness, no petition. The people are dying of thirst and they know it.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders the quarreling with *eloidoreito ho laos pros Mōusēn* (ἐλοιδορεῖτο ὁ λαὸς πρὸς Μωυσῆν, "the people reviled/abused Moses"), using *loidoreō* (λοιδορέω, "to revile, to abuse verbally") — a stronger term than the Hebrew *riv*, suggesting outright verbal assault rather than legal disputation.

**v.3 — THE ACCUSATION OF MURDER (Heb. *vayyitsma sham ha'am lammayim vayyallen ha'am al-Mosheh vayyomer lammah zeh he'elitanu mimMitsrayim lehamit oti uvanai umiknai batsama*, וַיִּצְמָ֨א שָׁ֤ם הָעָם֙ לַמַּ֔יִם וַיָּ֥לֶן הָעָ֖ם עַל־מֹשֶׁ֑ה וַיֹּ֗אמֶר לָ֤מָּה זֶּה֙ הֶעֱלִיתָ֣נוּ מִמִּצְרַ֔יִם לְהָמִ֥ית אֹתִ֛י וְאֶת־בָּנַ֥י וְאֶת־מִקְנַ֖י בַּצָּמָֽא):** The verb *vayyitsma* (ויצמא, from *tsama'*, צמא, "to be thirsty") and the noun *tsama'* (צמא, "thirst") frame the crisis. The verb *vayyallen* (וילן, from *lun*, לון, "to murmur, to grumble") is the same complaint-verb from 15:24 and 16:2 — the wilderness grumbling has become a fixed pattern.

The accusation is nearly identical to 16:3 but now escalated. In chapter 16, they accused Moses of bringing them out *lehamit* (להמית, "to kill") the whole assembly with hunger. Now the charge is killing "me and my children and my livestock" (*oti uvanai umiknai*, אתי ואת בני ואת מקני) with thirst (*batsama'*, בצמא). The shift from collective (*kol-haqqahal*, "the whole assembly") to individual-familial (*oti uvanai*, "me and my children") makes the accusation more personal, more desperate. The inclusion of *miqneh* (מקנה, "livestock, cattle") reveals the practical reality: the animals are dying too. This is not merely human discomfort; the entire community — human and animal — is perishing.

**v.4 — MOSES BETWEEN THE PEOPLE AND GOD (Heb. *vayyits'aq Mosheh el-YHWH lemor mah e'eseh la'am hazzeh od me'at usqaluni*, וַיִּצְעַ֤ק מֹשֶׁה֙ אֶל־יְהוָ֣ה לֵאמֹ֔ר מָ֥ה אֶעֱשֶׂ֖ה לָעָ֣ם הַזֶּ֑ה ע֥וֹד מְעַ֖ט וּסְקָלֻֽנִי):** The verb *vayyits'aq* (ויצעק, from *tsa'aq*, צעק, "to cry out, to scream") is the same root Israel used in Egypt when they cried out under oppression (2:23). Now Moses cries out to God under the pressure of the people he liberated. The irony is structural: the liberator is now threatened by the liberated.

The phrase *od me'at usqaluni* (עוד מעט וסקלני, "a little more and they will stone me") reveals that the quarrel has reached the threshold of mob violence. Stoning (*saqal*, סקל) is the characteristic form of communal execution in ancient Israel — a collective act in which the whole community participates in the killing (cf. Lev. 20:2; 24:14; Num. 15:35-36; 1 Kings 21:10). Moses is not exaggerating; the people are on the edge of lynching their leader. This is the nadir of the wilderness relationship: the people whom Moses rescued from death are now about to kill Moses.

**vv.5-6 — THE ROCK AT HOREB (Heb. *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh avor lifnei ha'am veqach ittekha miziqnei Yisra'el umattkha asher hikkita bo et-haYe'or qach beyadekha vehalakhta; hinni omed lefanekha sham al-hatsur beChorev vehikkita vatsur veyatse'u mimmennu mayim veshatah ha'am vayyaas ken Mosheh le'einei ziqnei Yisra'el*, וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֗ה עֲבֹר֙ לִפְנֵ֣י הָעָ֔ם וְקַ֥ח אִתְּךָ֖ מִזִּקְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל וּמַטְּךָ֗ אֲשֶׁ֨ר הִכִּ֤יתָ בּוֹ֙ אֶת־הַיְאֹ֔ר קַ֥ח בְּיָדְךָ֖ וְהָלָֽכְתָּ הִנְנִ֣י עֹמֵד֩ לְפָנֶ֨יךָ שָּׁ֥ם עַֽל־הַצּוּר֮ בְּחֹרֵב֒ וְהִכִּ֣יתָ בַצּ֗וּר וְיָצְא֥וּ מִמֶּ֛נּוּ מַ֖יִם וְשָׁתָ֣ה הָעָ֑ם וַיַּ֤עַשׂ כֵּן֙ מֹשֶׁ֔ה לְעֵינֵ֖י זִקְנֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל):** YHWH's instructions are precise and layered with significance. The command *avor lifnei ha'am* (עבר לפני העם, "pass/go before the people") — using *avar* (עבר, "to pass over, to cross") — places Moses in front of the people, between them and the rock, in a position of mediatory leadership. He is to take *miziqnei Yisra'el* (מזקני ישראל, "some of the elders of Israel") as witnesses. The elders see what happens at the rock; the people receive the water. The miracle has an audience of verification.

The staff (*matteh*, מטה) is specifically identified as *asher hikkita bo et-haYe'or* (אשר הכית בו את היאר, "with which you struck the Nile"). This is the same staff from the plagues — the instrument that turned water to blood (7:17, 20) is now the instrument that brings water from stone. In Egypt, the staff turned water into a curse; in the wilderness, it turns rock into a blessing. The staff's identity bridges the exodus and the wilderness, connecting liberation and provision as two acts of the same God through the same instrument.

The most theologically stunning statement is *hinni omed lefanekha sham al-hatsur* (הנני עמד לפניך שם על הצור, "behold, I am standing before you there on the rock"). YHWH himself will be standing *on the rock* when Moses strikes it. The rock is not merely a geological feature; it is the location of divine presence. When Moses strikes the rock, he strikes the place where God stands. This imagery was not lost on later interpreters: Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 10:4 that "the rock was Christ" (*hē petra de ēn ho Christos*, ἡ πέτρα δὲ ἦν ὁ Χριστός) — reading the water-from-the-rock as a type of divine self-giving through suffering.

The word *tsur* (צור, "rock, cliff") is distinct from *sela'* (סלע, "crag, rock") used in the parallel Numbers 20 episode. *Tsur* is the harder, more massive formation — bedrock, not a boulder. It becomes a divine epithet: *tsur Yisra'el* (צור ישראל, "the Rock of Israel," Gen. 49:24; 2 Sam. 23:3), *hatsur* (הצור, "the Rock," Deut. 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31). That YHWH stands on *hatsur* and water flows when the rock is struck anticipates the theology of Deuteronomy 32, where YHWH *is* the Rock.

The mention of *Chorev* (חרב, "Horeb") — another name for Sinai or the region around Sinai — places this event geographically near the mountain of revelation. Israel has not yet arrived at Sinai (that happens in chapter 19), but Horeb is already present as a sacred location. Some scholars suggest Rephidim is the staging area at the base of the Sinai massif, with the rock being on an outlying spur of Horeb itself. The proximity is theologically significant: the God who will speak from Sinai already provides from Horeb's rock.

The phrase *le'einei ziqnei Yisra'el* (לעיני זקני ישראל, "before the eyes of the elders of Israel") — literally "to the eyes of" — emphasizes that the miracle was witnessed by Israel's leadership. This is a public, verifiable event, not a private theophany.

The LXX renders *hikkita* (הכית, "you struck") with *epataxas* (ἐπάταξας, "you struck/smote"), the same verb used for the plagues, reinforcing the connection between the plague-staff and the provision-staff.

**v.7 — MASSAH AND MERIBAH — THE NAMING OF CRISIS (Heb. *vayyiqra shem hammaqqom Massah uMerivah al-riv benei Yisra'el ve'al nassotam et-YHWH lemor hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin*, וַיִּקְרָא֙ שֵׁ֣ם הַמָּק֔וֹם מַסָּ֖ה וּמְרִיבָ֑ה עַל־רִ֣יב ׀ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל וְעַ֨ל נַסֹּתָ֤ם אֶת־יְהוָה֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר הֲיֵ֧שׁ יְהוָ֛ה בְּקִרְבֵּ֖נוּ אִם־אָֽיִן):** The dual naming — *Massah* (מסה, from *nasah*, נסה, "to test") and *Merivah* (מריבה, from *riv*, ריב, "to quarrel") — memorializes the two sins of this episode: quarreling with Moses and testing YHWH. These names will echo across the biblical tradition as paradigmatic warnings. Deuteronomy 6:16: "Do not put YHWH your God to the test as you tested him at Massah." Psalm 95:8-9: "Do not harden your hearts as at Meribah, as on the day of Massah in the wilderness, when your fathers tested me." Hebrews 3:7-11 quotes Psalm 95 as a warning to the Christian community. The place-names become moral categories — types of faithlessness.

The question that names the place is devastating in its simplicity: *hayesh YHWH beqirbenu im-ayin* (היש יהוה בקרבנו אם אין, "Is YHWH among us, or not?"). The word *qerev* (קרב, "midst, interior, inward part") suggests intimate, interior presence — not "is God somewhere in the sky?" but "is God *here*, in our midst, in the center of our community?" The alternative *ayin* (אין, "nothing, absence, non-existence") is stark: the people are asking whether YHWH is present or simply absent. After the plagues, the sea-crossing, the pillar of cloud and fire, the sweetening of Marah's water, and the manna — after all of this — they still ask: is God here? The question reveals that miracles do not produce faith; they only provide occasions for it. Evidence does not compel trust. The people have seen everything and believe nothing.

This question is the theological heart of the entire wilderness narrative. Every crisis from Exodus 15 to Numbers 20 is a variation on this theme: is YHWH among us or not? The tabernacle (chapters 25-40) will be God's architectural answer to this question — a physical structure in which the divine presence *dwells* (*shakan*, שכן) in Israel's midst (*betokham*, בתוכם, 25:8). But the people ask the question before the answer is built.

**v.8 — AMALEK ATTACKS (Heb. *vayyavo Amaleq vayyillahem im-Yisra'el biRfidim*, וַיָּבֹ֖א עֲמָלֵ֑ק וַיִּלָּ֥חֶם עִם־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל בִּרְפִידִֽם):** The narrative shifts abruptly from theological crisis to military crisis. Amalek (*Amaleq*, עמלק) is identified in Genesis 36:12 as a grandson of Esau, making the Amalekites distant kin of Israel — this is fratricide, not foreign aggression. The Amalekites were a semi-nomadic people who ranged across the Negev and northern Sinai, raiding vulnerable populations. Deuteronomy 25:17-18 adds a crucial detail absent from Exodus: Amalek attacked Israel's rearguard — the stragglers, the exhausted, the weak — "when you were weary and worn out, and they had no fear of God." The attack was cowardly, targeting the most vulnerable.

The verb *vayyillahem* (וילחם, from *lacham*, לחם, "to fight, to wage war") — in the niphal, "he fought against" — introduces the first military engagement in Israel's history as a nation. They have been freed from Egypt, fed in the wilderness, given water from the rock — and now they must fight. The shift from provision-crisis to warfare-crisis signals a new phase: Israel must not only trust God for bread and water but also for victory against enemies.

The juxtaposition is narratively deliberate: immediately after the people ask "Is YHWH among us or not?" (v.7), an enemy attacks (v.8). The question of divine presence is about to be answered on a battlefield.

**v.9 — JOSHUA'S DEBUT AND THE STAFF OF GOD (Heb. *vayyomer Mosheh el-Yehoshu'a bechar-lanu anashim vetse hillachem ba'Amaleq machar anokhi nitsav al-rosh haggiv'ah umatteh ha'Elohim beyadi*, וַיֹּ֨אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֤ה אֶל־יְהוֹשֻׁ֨עַ֙ בְּחַר־לָ֣נוּ אֲנָשִׁ֔ים וְצֵ֖א הִלָּחֵ֣ם בַּעֲמָלֵ֑ק מָחָ֗ר אָנֹכִ֤י נִצָּב֙ עַל־רֹ֣אשׁ הַגִּבְעָ֔ה וּמַטֵּ֥ה הָאֱלֹהִ֖ים בְּיָדִֽי):** This is the first appearance of *Yehoshu'a* (יהושע, "Joshua") in the biblical narrative. The name means "YHWH saves" or "YHWH is salvation" — from *yasha'* (ישע, "to save, to deliver") prefixed with the divine name. He appears without introduction, without genealogy, without explanation — as if the reader already knows him. His sudden appearance in a military context foreshadows his future role as Israel's war-leader in the conquest. Moses delegates the battlefield to Joshua but retains the intercessory position above.

The phrase *bechar-lanu anashim* (בחר לנו אנשים, "choose men for us") uses *bachar* (בחר, "to choose, to select"), the verb of election — the same root used for God's choice of Israel (Deut. 7:6) and later of David (1 Sam. 16:8-10). Joshua must select fighting men from a nation of former slaves who have never held weapons. This is an army created from scratch.

The staff is now called *matteh ha'Elohim* (מטה האלהים, "the staff of God") rather than *matteh Mosheh* ("the staff of Moses") — a significant title shift. The instrument that struck the Nile, parted the sea, and struck the rock is not Moses' tool; it is God's. Moses will hold it, but the power is divine.

Moses' position — *al-rosh haggiv'ah* (על ראש הגבעה, "on the top of the hill") — places him above the battlefield in a posture of intercessory authority. He does not fight; he intercedes. The leader's role is not to wield the sword but to wield the staff of God. This establishes a pattern that will define Israel's theology of warfare: battles are won not by military prowess alone but by divine intervention mediated through human intercession.

**vv.10-12 — THE RAISED HANDS AND THE STONE SEAT (Heb. *vayyaas Yehoshu'a ka'asher amar-lo Mosheh lehillachem ba'Amaleq uMosheh Aharon veChur alu rosh haggiv'ah; vehayah ka'asher yarim Mosheh yado vegavar Yisra'el vekha'asher yaniach yado vegavar Amaleq; videi Mosheh kevedim vayyiqchu even vayyasimu tachtav vayyeshev aleiha veAharon veChur tamkhu veyadav mizzeh echad umizzeh echad vayehi yadav emunah ad-bo hashamesh*, וַיַּ֣עַשׂ יְהוֹשֻׁ֗עַ כַּאֲשֶׁ֤ר אָמַר־לוֹ֙ מֹשֶׁ֔ה לְהִלָּחֵ֖ם בַּעֲמָלֵ֑ק וּמֹשֶׁה֙ אַהֲרֹ֣ן וְח֔וּר עָל֖וּ רֹ֥אשׁ הַגִּבְעָֽה וְהָיָ֗ה כַּאֲשֶׁ֨ר יָרִ֥ים מֹשֶׁ֛ה יָד֖וֹ וְגָבַ֣ר יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל וְכַאֲשֶׁ֥ר יָנִ֛יחַ יָד֖וֹ וְגָבַ֥ר עֲמָלֵֽק וִידֵ֤י מֹשֶׁה֙ כְּבֵדִ֔ים וַיִּקְחוּ־אֶ֛בֶן וַיָּשִׂ֥ימוּ תַחְתָּ֖יו וַיֵּ֣שֶׁב עָלֶ֑יהָ וְאַהֲרֹ֨ן וְח֜וּר תָּֽמְכ֣וּ בְיָדָ֗יו מִזֶּ֤ה אֶחָד֙ וּמִזֶּ֣ה אֶחָ֔ד וַיְהִ֥י יָדָ֛יו אֱמוּנָ֖ה עַד־בֹּ֥א הַשָּֽׁמֶשׁ):** This passage is one of the most visually iconic in the Hebrew Bible and its theological implications are immense.

*Chur* (חור, "Hur") appears here for the first time, alongside Aaron. Jewish tradition (Josephus, *Antiquities* 3.2.4; Targum pseudo-Jonathan on 24:14) identifies Hur as the son of Caleb and Miriam, making him Moses' nephew. He will reappear in 24:14, where Moses leaves him and Aaron in charge during the Sinai ascent.

The verb *yarim* (ירים, from *rum*, רום, "to raise, to lift up") describes Moses' gesture. When his hand (*yado*, ידו — some manuscripts read the singular *yado*, others the plural *yadav*; the Masoretic text oscillates between singular and plural in these verses) is raised, Israel *gavar* (גבר, "prevails, is strong, overcomes"); when he *yaniach* (יניח, from *nuach*, נוח, "to rest, to set down, to lower") his hand, Amalek prevails. The correlation is direct and mechanistic — the battle's outcome is determined not by tactics on the ground but by posture on the hill.

What does the raised hand mean? The Mishnah (Rosh Hashanah 3:8) asks this question directly and answers: "Could the hands of Moses win the war or lose the war? Rather, when Israel looked upward and directed their hearts to their Father in heaven, they prevailed; when they did not, they fell." The Mishnah insists the raised hands are not magic but a direction-marker: they cause the people to look up toward God. The gesture mediates faith.

The word *kevedim* (כבדים, "heavy," from *kaved*, כבד, "to be heavy") applied to Moses' hands echoes the same root used of Pharaoh's heart (*kaved lev Par'oh*, "Pharaoh's heart was heavy/hardened," 7:14; 8:15; 9:7). But where Pharaoh's heaviness was moral obstinacy, Moses' heaviness is physical exhaustion. The same root, opposite meanings: one is the weight of stubbornness against God; the other is the weight of sustained prayer to God.

The solution is communal: Aaron and Hur *tamkhu* (תמכו, from *tamakh*, תמך, "to support, to uphold, to sustain") Moses' hands. The verb *tamakh* appears in Psalm 54:6 ("YHWH is the one who sustains my life") and Isaiah 42:1 ("Here is my servant, whom I uphold [*etmakh-bo*]"). Intercession is not a solitary act; it requires a community of support. Moses cannot win the battle alone — not on the battlefield (Joshua fights) and not on the hilltop (Aaron and Hur hold his arms).

The extraordinary final clause — *vayehi yadav emunah ad-bo hashamesh* (ויהי ידיו אמונה עד בא השמש, "and his hands were *emunah* until the setting of the sun") — uses the word *emunah* (אמונה), usually translated "faithfulness" or "steadiness." The root is *aman* (אמן, "to be firm, to be faithful, to be trustworthy") — the root of "amen." Moses' hands were literally "faithfulness" — steady, firm, unwavering. The word *emunah* here bridges the physical (steady hands) and the theological (faithful trust). It is the same word used in Habakkuk 2:4: *tsaddiq be'emunato yichyeh* (צדיק באמונתו יחיה, "the righteous shall live by his faithfulness"), the verse Paul quotes in Romans 1:17 and Galatians 3:11 to ground the doctrine of justification by faith. The hands of Moses are the physical embodiment of *emunah* — sustained, communal, embodied faithfulness that holds on until sundown.

**v.13 — JOSHUA'S VICTORY (Heb. *vayyachalosh Yehoshu'a et-Amaleq ve'et-ammo lefi-charev*, וַיַּחֲלֹ֧שׁ יְהוֹשֻׁ֛עַ אֶת־עֲמָלֵ֥ק וְאֶת־עַמּ֖וֹ לְפִי־חָֽרֶב):** The verb *vayyachalosh* (ויחלש, from *chalash*, חלש, "to weaken, to disable, to overwhelm") is rare — it does not mean "annihilate" or "destroy" but "weaken, disable." The victory is decisive but not total; Amalek survives to fight again (hence the perpetual-war declaration in v.16). The phrase *lefi-charev* (לפי חרב, literally "to the mouth of the sword") is a standard Hebrew idiom for death in battle — the sword "devours" or "consumes" its victims (cf. 2 Sam. 2:26; Isa. 1:20). The LXX uses *etrepsen* (ἔτρεψεν, "he routed"), which implies a military turning — the enemy put to flight rather than slaughtered.

**v.14 — THE WRITTEN MEMORIAL AND THE BLOTTING OUT OF AMALEK (Heb. *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh ketov zot zikkaron bassefer vesim be'oznei Yehoshu'a ki-machoh emcheh et-zekher Amaleq mittachat hashamayim*, וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֗ה כְּתֹ֨ב זֹ֤את זִכָּרוֹן֙ בַּסֵּ֔פֶר וְשִׂ֖ים בְּאׇזְנֵ֣י יְהוֹשֻׁ֑עַ כִּֽי־מָחֹ֤ה אֶמְחֶה֙ אֶת־זֵ֣כֶר עֲמָלֵ֔ק מִתַּ֖חַת הַשָּׁמָֽיִם):** This verse is remarkable for several reasons. First, it is one of the few explicit references to writing in the Torah — God commands Moses to *ketov* (כתב, "write") this event as a *zikkaron* (זכרון, "memorial, remembrance") in *hassefer* (הספר, "the document/book/scroll"). The definite article *ha-* suggests a specific, known document — not just any writing surface. This has been interpreted as "the Book of the Wars of YHWH" (cf. Num. 21:14), a proto-biblical war chronicle, or simply as the Torah scroll itself.

Second, Moses is to *sim be'oznei Yehoshu'a* (שים באזני יהושע, "place it in the ears of Joshua") — that is, recite it aloud to Joshua. The command bridges the written and the oral: it must be both inscribed and spoken. Joshua hears the oracle because he will lead the ongoing war against Amalek.

Third, the oracle itself — *machoh emcheh et-zekher Amaleq mittachat hashamayim* (מחה אמחה את זכר עמלק מתחת השמים, "I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven") — uses the infinitive absolute construction *machoh emcheh* (מחה אמחה, "blotting I will blot out") for emphatic intensification. The verb *machah* (מחה, "to wipe out, to blot out, to erase") is the same verb used of the flood — God "blotted out" (*vayyimach*, וימח) every living thing from the face of the ground (Gen. 7:23) — and of sin-erasure: "blot out (*mecheh*) my transgressions" (Ps. 51:3). The irony is that YHWH commands the *writing* of a memorial (*zikkaron*) that announces the *erasure* of a memory (*zekher*). The memorial of Amalek's destruction is preserved; the memory of Amalek itself is to be obliterated. Writing preserves what blotting-out destroys — and the two acts are commanded in the same breath.

The word *zekher* (זכר, "memory, remembrance, mention") differs from *zikkaron* (זכרון, "memorial") — both derive from the root *zakhar* (זכר, "to remember"), but *zekher* is the ongoing trace of existence, the fact that a name is spoken and a people remembered. To blot out the *zekher* of Amalek is to erase them from history, from narrative, from human consciousness. This command is repeated in Deuteronomy 25:19, where Israel is instructed to carry it out after settling the land.

**v.15 — *YHWH-NISSI* — "YHWH IS MY BANNER" (Heb. *vayyiven Mosheh mizbeach vayyiqra shemo YHWH Nissi*, וַיִּ֥בֶן מֹשֶׁ֖ה מִזְבֵּ֑חַ וַיִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמ֖וֹ יְהוָ֥ה ׀ נִסִּֽי):** Moses builds an altar (*mizbeach*, מזבח, from *zavach*, זבח, "to slaughter, to sacrifice") — this is only the second altar explicitly built in Exodus (after the one in 24:4, though chronologically this is first). The naming of the altar *YHWH Nissi* (יהוה נסי, "YHWH is my banner/standard") uses *nes* (נס, "banner, standard, ensign, signal-pole"). A *nes* was a pole or standard raised high on a hill as a rallying point for troops — a visible signal around which warriors gathered for battle (cf. Isa. 5:26; 11:10, 12; 49:22; Jer. 4:6). By naming the altar "YHWH is my banner," Moses declares that YHWH himself is the rallying standard of Israel's warfare. The army does not fight under Moses' flag or Joshua's — they fight under YHWH's.

The name also resonates with the raised hands on the hilltop: Moses' uplifted hands holding the staff of God functioned as the visible *nes* — the signal that the battle was YHWH's. The altar memorializes the truth that the hilltop posture revealed: Israel's banner is not military power but divine presence.

**v.16 — THE ENIGMATIC OATH (Heb. *vayyomer ki-yad al-kes Yah milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor*, וַיֹּ֗אמֶר כִּֽי־יָד֙ עַל־כֵּ֣ס יָ֔הּ מִלְחָמָ֥ה לַיהוָ֖ה בַּעֲמָלֵ֑ק מִדֹּ֖ר דֹּֽר):** This final verse is one of the most textually difficult in the Torah. The phrase *ki-yad al-kes Yah* (כי יד על כס יה) is compressed and ambiguous. The word *kes* (כס) appears to be a shortened or defective form of *kisse'* (כסא, "throne"); the name *Yah* (יה) is the abbreviated form of YHWH (as in *Hallelu-Yah*, "Praise Yah"). Literally: "For a hand [is] upon the throne of Yah" — but whose hand? And what does the gesture mean?

Several interpretations: (1) Moses raises his hand toward the throne of God and swears an oath — the hand on the throne is a swearing gesture, an oath taken in God's name. (2) YHWH himself raises his hand to his own throne, swearing by himself (cf. Gen. 22:16; Isa. 45:23). (3) The "hand" is Amalek's hand raised *against* God's throne — Amalek's attack on Israel is an assault on YHWH's sovereignty.

The Targum Onqelos paraphrases: "An oath has been sworn from before the throne of YHWH's glory." The midrashic tradition (Tanchuma, Ki Tetse 11) connects the defective spelling of both *kes* (lacking the final *aleph* of *kisse'*) and *Yah* (lacking the final *heh-vav* of *YHWH*) with the incompleteness of God's name and throne as long as Amalek exists — God's name is not whole and God's throne is not complete until Amalek is destroyed. This is a daring theological claim: the existence of unremediated evil diminishes the fullness of divine sovereignty.

The declaration *milchamah laYHWH ba'Amaleq middor dor* (מלחמה ליהוה בעמלק מדר דר, "war for YHWH against Amalek from generation to generation") establishes a perpetual state of war. This is not a single battle but an ongoing cosmic conflict. The phrase *middor dor* (מדר דר, "from generation to generation") — note the absence of the usual connecting *le-* or *el-* between the two *dor* words, making the phrase itself feel compressed and urgent — means that the war against Amalek outlasts any single generation. Amalek becomes a symbol in later tradition: Haman the Agagite (Esther 3:1) is identified as a descendant of Agag, king of the Amalekites (1 Sam. 15:8), making the Purim story a continuation of the Exodus 17 war. In rabbinic thought, Amalek represents the archetypal enemy of God's people — not merely a historical nation but a spiritual principle of predatory violence against the vulnerable.

The LXX renders this verse with interpretive expansion: *hoti en cheiri kryphaía polemei kyrios epi Amalēk apo geneōn eis geneas* (ὅτι ἐν χειρὶ κρυφαίᾳ πολεμεῖ κύριος ἐπὶ Αμαληκ ἀπὸ γενεῶν εἰς γενεάς, "because with a hidden hand the Lord wages war against Amalek from generations to generations"). The addition of *kryphaía* (κρυφαία, "hidden, secret") is a significant interpretive move: the hand is *hidden* — God's warfare against Amalek operates through concealed providence, not always visible to human eyes.

---

**The theology of Exodus 17 — presence, intercession, and the shape of faithfulness:**

This chapter divides into two crises that mirror each other and together answer the central question of Israel's wilderness existence.

**1. "Is YHWH among us or not?" — the question the wilderness asks.** The water crisis (vv.1-7) crystallizes the deepest anxiety of the wilderness: divine absence. The people have been led by God to a place with no water, and the absence of water becomes a sign of the absence of God. The logic is experiential: if God were here, we would not be dying. The irony is that God led them there precisely to demonstrate presence through provision. The rock-that-gives-water answers the question physically: YHWH stands on the rock, and water flows. But the answer is not self-evident — it requires faith to see the water as proof of presence rather than as belated damage control.

**2. The rock, the staff, and the pattern of reversal.** The staff that struck the Nile and brought plague now strikes the rock and brings life. The same instrument, wielded by the same man, at the same God's command — but the outcome is inverted. Egypt's water became blood; Horeb's rock becomes water. The wilderness systematically undoes Egypt: what was cursed in the land of slavery is blessed in the place of freedom. The staff is the hinge between these two worlds.

**3. Moses as intercessor — the body as prayer.** The Amalek battle (vv.8-16) introduces a theology of intercession that is profoundly physical. Moses does not pray with words (no prayer is recorded); he prays with his body — arms raised, staff held high, body supported by stone and human hands. The effectiveness of intercession is visible and measurable: when the arms are up, Israel wins; when they drop, Israel loses. This is not sympathetic magic but embodied faith — the human body positioned as a conduit for divine power. The exhaustion of the intercessor (heavy hands) is as real as the exhaustion of the soldier. Intercession costs something.

**4. The community of sustaining.** Moses cannot intercede alone. Aaron and Hur must hold his arms. The stone must support his body. Intercession requires a community of supporters who themselves may never be named in the victory narrative. This is one of the earliest and most vivid images of shared spiritual labor in the Bible: the intercessor needs intercessors. The one who holds up others must be held up himself.

**5. Amalek and the incompleteness of redemption.** The perpetual war against Amalek (v.16) introduces the uncomfortable truth that deliverance from Egypt does not mean the end of conflict. There are enemies after Pharaoh. The wilderness is not safe. The promised land must be fought for. Amalek represents the ongoing reality of evil that targets the vulnerable (Deut. 25:18) and that will not be resolved within any single generation. The defective spelling of God's throne-name — *kes Yah* rather than *kisse' YHWH* — suggests that the world remains incomplete, God's sovereignty remains contested, as long as Amalek's principle endures. The altar YHWH-Nissi is planted in the ground as a permanent declaration: the war is YHWH's, and it will not end until the banner flies over a world where the predatory destruction of the weak has no place.

— *PNEUMA*
